titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Fritz Eichenberg Papers
titlestmt.author: Manuscripts and Archives Staff

controlnote.p: This finding aid, which is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0., has a MARC record in Yale's ILS with the following bib number: 1031931.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday September 1, 2020 at 23:02

did.unittitle: Fritz Eichenberg papers
did.abstract: The papers consist of correspondence; professional, travel, and teaching files; financial and legal papers; printed material; and memorabilia documenting the professional life of graphic artist Fritz Eichenberg.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Original audiovisual materials, as well as preservation and duplicating masters, may not be played. Researchers must consult use copies, or if none exist must pay for a use copy, which is retained by the repository.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of the Estate of Fritz Eichenberg, 1991, 1992, 1995, 1996, and 1999; transferred from Manuscripts and Archives to Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library, 2014.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The copyright and literary rights to the papers of Fritz Eichenberg reside in the Fritz Eichenberg Trust. Permission to publish must be obtained from the trustees. Requests should be directed to the Trustees of the Fritz Eichenberg Trust, c/o VAGA, 111 Broadway, Suite 1006, New York, NY 10006, (212)736-6666 - info@vagarights.com.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Fritz Eichenberg Papers, Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library, Yale University Library.

relatedmaterial.head: Associated Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Additional graphic works and artist's tools in the collection of Fritz Eichenberg Designs and Art Work (Call # AOB 23), Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library, Yale University. Requires curatorial permission before use.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Fritz Eichenberg was born in Cologne, Germany, on October 24, 1901. He studied at the School of Applied Arts and was a lithographic apprentice before being accepted as a master student at the Academy of Graphic Arts in Leipzig. Eichenberg moved to Berlin in 1923 but decided to leave for the United States when Hitler came to power in 1933. He taught at the New School for Social Research in New York, worked on the Federal Art Project, and received commissions from the Limited Editions Club and the Heritage Press. Eichenberg became well known for his prints and illustrations of many of the great classics. He started teaching at Pratt Institute in 1947 and held a number of positions while there. In 1966 Eichenberg became chairman of the Art Department of the University of Rhode Island in Kingston, a position he resigned in 1971. He traveled extensively in semi-official capacities, to the Soviet Union for the State Department and to Southeast Asia for the J. D. Rockefeller III Fund. In addition, he wrote definitive works on graphic arts. Fritz Eichenberg died in Peace Dale, Rhode Island, on November 30, 1990.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The papers consist of correspondence; professional, travel, and teaching files; financial and legal papers; printed material; and memorabilia documenting the professional life of graphic artist Fritz Eichenberg.

did.unittitle: Inventory
F.E. letters (manuscript draft copies)
A - D: incoming letters and greeting cards w/ messages from Friends, students, colleagues, some business associates
D - H: incoming letters and greeting cards w/ messages from Friends, students, colleagues, family (Toni, Margaret, Tim), some business associates
H - K: incoming letters and greeting cards w/ messages from Friends, students, colleagues, family (Susan "Butjen" Jensen), some business associates
L - N: incoming letters and greeting cards w/ messages from Friends, students, colleagues, some business associates
N - R: incoming letters and greeting cards w/ messages from Friends, students, colleagues, some business associates
R - T: incoming letters and greeting cards w/ messages from Friends, students, colleagues, family (Georg Schetze-Forster) some business associates
T - Z: incoming letters and greeting cards w/ messages from Friends, students, colleagues, some business associates
Artists and Faculty unsorted incoming letters and greeting cards w/ messages, similar to above A - Z files
Children's letters , admirers
Christmas: Original Prints these are greeting cards, some w/ original prints, none by F.E., similar to many such cards in above A - Z files
Dorothy Day incoming letters and note cards w/ photocopies and F. E. ms transcriptions, 8 x 10 b and w portrait of D.D.
F.E. photocopies of Folder 16
Haverford incoming letters from John Spielman, David Fraser
Private Correspondence incoming letters from students, admirers, friends, unsorted, similar to above A - Z files
Dark blue Accogrip binder w/ incoming letters from admirers, Friends, many re F. E.'s illustrations for Catholic Worker
Black Accogrip binder w/ incoming letters similar to Folder 1
"Scrapbook" softbound page dummy with some 75 incoming letters laid in including 2 from Albert Einstein, 4 from Alvin Johnson, 2 from Leonard Baskin
Unidentified, incoming letters and greeting cards w/ messages authors not identified by sorter, writers include Butjen, James Forrest, Hans Halbey, John Spielman, Sister Thoma, Tolman Kura Int'l
Unidentified , incoming letters and greeting cards w/ messages authors not identified by sorter, writers include, Hans Halbey, Alexander Theroux, Judith C. Jaidinger, Shelden Hurt
Empty envelopes separated from letters throughout files
Abbeville re F.E.'s DANCE OF DEATH

Fellowship of Reconciliation incoming letters from James Forrest

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addition inventory by Carol King 1992 Dec
Received from: Estate of Fritz Eichenberg, 1992 Sep 22

Additional Material

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This accession is unprocessed and has no formal arrangement. The box listing is often very general, highlighting main categories of material within each box.
